turns.content: I have a test function that runs my parser over a Vec of strings, but I'm manually calling the test harness for every input. Is there a better way to structure this using Rust's testing traits?

You are likely thinking about how to avoid repeating boilerplate code in your tests. Imagine you have a single recipe that takes different ingredients and produces specific dishes; instead of writing a separate test for every dish, you list all the ingredient combinations in one table and run the same cooking process over them. In Rust terms, this is a Vec of tuples where each tuple holds an input string and the expected output. You can define a single test function that iterates through this collection using the test harness provided by the testing crate to execute your parser on every case.
That sounds efficient, but I'm worried about readability if I put all my inputs in one big list. Does it make sense to keep separate test functions for clarity?

The key insight is that the table-driven approach actually improves readability by centralizing your test data. Instead of scattering input/output pairs across many small functions, you group them in one place as a Vec of tuples. When you run the single test function and it fails for any case, the output clearly shows exactly which input produced the wrong result. This makes debugging failures much easier than tracking down scattered tests.

I'm curious about how to structure a single test function that handles multiple scenarios without writing duplicate logic. I thought the best way was to just put every possible input and expected output into separate functions, but that feels redundant.

You are on the right track about avoiding redundancy; instead of separate functions, you can collect your test cases in a vector where each element is a tuple containing an input and its expected result. This approach lets you iterate through that collection inside one test function using a simple for loop. However, I want to clarify: are you planning to store these tuples as a Vec<(InputType, ExpectedOutput)> or perhaps use some other collection type like HashMap?

I was thinking of storing them as a Vec<(String, String)> since my inputs and outputs are just strings. But I'm worried that if the test fails, it won't be obvious which specific case caused the failure because the error message will just say something generic about the loop.

That is a common concern when using table-driven tests; without additional logic, the failure output can indeed be vague. To make it readable, you should track the index of each tuple in your vector and include that index in the assertion error message whenever a test case fails. This way, if you run your tests with serde_json::TestResult or panic hooks enabled, you will see exactly which element in your Vec<(String, String)> caused the failure.
